documents.content: mpared the degradation of unstabilized PVC with the degradation of PVC stabilized with calcium stearate, zinc stearate, and zinc chloride, which are common heat 3 stabilizers used to avoid the decomposition of PVC during melt processing. Arkis et al. [19] observed the effect of organotin as a stabilizer over PVC thermal degradation at 140°C, 160°C and 180°C and proved that it is a good heat stabilizer for plasticized PVC while not having any detrimental effects on mechanical properties [19]. On the other hand, according to Bacaloglu et al. [20,21], the presence of zinc or tin carboxylates can be responsible for a very fast discoloration of PVC – Zn or Sn carboxylates act as stabilizers until the corresponding halides are formed which marks the start of fast degradation due to the formation of Lewis acid sites by alkyl halides exhibiting a catalytic effect for further dehydrochlorination reaction. Most literature related to the thermal degradation of rigid unplasticized PVC during artificially accelerated tests deals with the degradation mechanisms at high temperatures (120-200°C range) [22–26], i.e. significantly different than typical environmental conditions encountered in heritage institutions. Less attention has been paid to artificial thermal degradation at moderate temperatures and high relative humidity (RH) that could be more representative of the conditions of the museum environment. Therefore, in this study we focused on unplasticized PVC stabilized with organotin (denoted PVC1) which was artificially degraded at lower temperatures ranging from 60°C to 80°C with a wide RH range of up to 60%. Analysis of the degraded samples was done by Raman spectroscopy, ATR-FTIR, spectrophotometric evaluation of color changes, UV-Vis, SEC, contact angle measurements, XPS, NMR, EPR and dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) to recognize the fundamental link between chemical degradation and changes of macroscopic mechanical properties. 2. Experimental: 2.1. Materials Rigid tran

on to avoid irreversible ecological damage by stemming the tide of microplastics entering the environment. Climate change conditions turn plastics into more mobile, persistent, and hazardous pollutants. This is done by speeding up plastic breakdown into mipcroplastics - microscopic fragments of plastic - spreading them considerable distances, and increasing exposure and impact within the environment This is set to worsen as both plastic manufacturing and climate effects increase. Global annual plastic production rose 200-fold between 1950 and 2023. The authors urge eliminating non-essential single-use plastics (which account for 35% of production), limiting virgin plastic production, and creating international standards for making plastics reusable and recyclable. “Plastic pollution and the climate are co-crises that intensify each other. They also have origins—and solutions—in common,” said lead author Professor Frank Kelly, from Imperial’s School of Public Health. “We urgently need a coordinated international approach to stop end-of-life plastics from building up in the environment.” Joint crises The researchers conducted a comprehensive review of existing evidence that highlights how the climate crisis worsens the impact of plastic pollution. Rising temperatures, humidity, and UV exposure all boost the breakdown of plastics. Furthermore, extreme storms, floods, and winds can increase fragmentation as well as dispersal of plastic waste – with six billion tons and rising – into landfill, aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems, atmospheric environments, and food webs. There are growing concerns about the persistence, spread, and accumulation of microplastics that can disturb nutrient cycles in aquatic ecosystems, reduce soil health, and crop yields. They also adversely affect feeding, reproduction, and the behaviour of organisms that are capable of ingesting them, should levels exceed safe thresholds. Microplastics can also act as ‘Trojan horses’ to transfer other conta

age to artworks made of PVC from the National Museum in Krakow (Poland). PVC can be produced in two formulations – plasticized and unplasticized. Unplasticized PVC is hard and rigid, examples of which are PVC objects from the Postcards 1968-1974 collection in Harvard Art Museums [4], while, by contrast, plasticized PVC is softer and more flexible, hence used more commonly in toys, design items and sculpture arts. PVC objects are degraded through two processes: degradation of PVC polymer chain and plasticizer loss [9,10]. PVC polymer degrades through dehydrochlorination, in which the polymer is converted to a hydrocarbon with a conjugated polyene system, releasing hydrochloric acid and accelerating the degradation process [11]. This conjugated polyene structure absorbs progressively longer wavelengths of light as the process continues, causing an increasing yellowing of PVC plastics over time [12]. Additionally, Shashoua et al. [13] reported cross-linking and chain scission reactions occurring during PVC degradation which alter the physical properties and lead to embrittlement. Plasticizer loss has been reviewed by many scientists (King et al. [10], Rijavec et al. [9], and Ledoux [4]). According to King et al. [10], plasticizer migration initially takes place through evaporation or absorption by a secondary material, such as a storage box, which further encourages the migration of more plasticizers to the surface due to a concentration gradient. Shashoua et al. [13] examined plasticizer loss by accelerated degradation of model plasticized PVC for 65 days and reported that the main source of the loss was due to plasticizer diffusion toward the surface. The degradation of PVC with other different additives was investigated by many researchers [14–17]. Gupper et al. [18] compared the degradation of unstabilized PVC with the degradation of PVC stabilized with calcium stearate, zinc stearate, and zinc chloride, which are common heat 3 stabilizers used to avoid the decompo

aforementioned changes did not lead to a notable decline in the mechanical properties, as evidenced by DMA and SEC measurements. EPR measurements demonstrated the formation of 1 radicals at 60°C, and in the sample degraded at 80°C the presence of radicals was evident. This indicates that a radical degradation mechanism cannot be excluded even at such low temperatures. Keywords: PVC thermal degradation, heritage objects, unplasticized PVC, spectroscopy, DMA 1. Introduction The extensive development of polymeric materials, where the main ingredients are synthetic polymers, is one of the twentieth century's biggest technological and industrial achievements. With a huge variety of forms, colors, and textures, the ease of molding, edge-banding, and modifying, commercial polymeric materials also found their way into artists’ studios. They have improved artistic expression in architecture, design, and artistry, enhancing modern art's capacity for interpretation and communication. Plastics of the twentieth century challenged their conservators with the problem of the degradation of polymeric materials and the resulting loss of objects’ value. The threat of irreversible loss of the tangible cultural heritage of the 20th and 21st centuries is a result of industrially manufactured plastics being intended for short-term use. They have in-built lifetimes ranging roughly from 1 year (supermarket carrier bag) to 50 years (u-PVC window frames), and, when exposed to unsuitable environments, they may exhibit rapid degradation, leading to the loss of their original properties. Another problem is related to the lack of control over material qualities, properties, and quantities resulting from modifications done by artists, which may have unexpected detrimental effects on object durability [1]. The deterioration of artifacts made from plastics was first reported in the literature in the late 1980s [2]. Estimates suggest between 15–30% of plastic objects in institutions across Europe are

[1]. The deterioration of artifacts made from plastics was first reported in the literature in the late 1980s [2]. Estimates suggest between 15–30% of plastic objects in institutions across Europe are in poor or unacceptable condition for display [3]. The deterioration of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) can be accelerated as a result of erroneous formulations. This phenomenon may give rise to changes that were not foreseen by either the artists or the collectors of these artworks. Yellowing or darkening of plastic works of art during exhibition and storage is recently one of the main issues in the case of objects made of PVC [4]. PVC is one of the “big five” large-tonnage plastics today. More than 5 million tons were manufactured in Europe in 2018, representing 10% of overall plastic production [5], and over 50 million tons were produced globally in 2022, amassing nearly 13% of all polymers [6]. A wide- ranging survey of museum collections in France, the Netherlands, and the UK was carried out in 2 the scope of the POPART project [7]. As a result, it was found that PVC was present in all collections and represented around 13% of all plastic objects. This study revealed that 68% of objects were in a good or fair state, 25% were in a state of significant decay and 7% were severely damaged. Cellulose acetate (CA), cellulose nitrate (CN), and PVC represented 40% of all objects in poor state [7]. Reporting of such problems has been prompted by museums worldwide. Keneghan et al. [3] published a survey of 7900 plastic objects from the Victoria and Albert Museum’s collection in London (England). 10% of surveyed objects suffered from degradation and showed evidence of chemical damage (classified as bloom, brittleness, or discoloration). Klisińska‐Kopacz et al. [8] reported chemical damage to artworks made of PVC from the National Museum in Krakow (Poland). PVC can be produced in two formulations – plasticized and unplasticized. Unplasticized PVC is hard and rigid, examples of which

R, EPR and dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) to recognize the fundamental link between chemical degradation and changes of macroscopic mechanical properties. 2. Experimental: 2.1. Materials Rigid transparent PVC foil of 0.2 mm thickness (produced in 2019 by Alfatherm s.p.a., herein denoted PVC1) was used to investigate the thermal degradation of unplasticized PVC. PVC1 is stabilized mainly with Irganox 1076 (octadecyl-3,5-bis(1,1-dimethyl ethyl)-4-hydroxy benzene propanoate – an ester of stearyl alcohol and propionic acid modified with large groups with significant steric hindrance providing thermo-oxidative stability to PVC). 2.2. Accelerated degradation For accelerated degradation, the PVC1 samples with the dimensions of 2x2 cm were placed in a climate chamber (BINDER Deutschland GmbH) at 60°C and 80°C with relative humidity control 4 (RH = 60%) for over 20 weeks. In order to investigate the humidity effect, another sample sets were degraded under low humidity regimes: at 60°C with RH = 20%, at 70oC with RH = 30%, and at 80°C without controlled humidity (using the Mollier diagram, the RH was no higher than 7%) for up to 22 weeks. The samples were also degraded in a dryer at high temperatures (120˚C and 140˚C) for up to five days to differentiate between degradation mechanisms at low and high temperatures. 2.3 Methods Raman spectra were measured using a Renishaw inVia Raman microscope 2000 system with the following parameters: an excitation wavelength of 514 nm, an acquisition time of 60 s, a laser power of 5% and spectral resolution of 4 cm-1, and a spectral range of 250–3500 cm-1. Each spectrum was the average of five scans obtained from different spots on the sample surface. Savitzky–Golay smoothing filter was used to minimize noise. The fundamental vibrations and associated rotation-vibration structures were studied in the mid-infrared (IR) region, 4000-500 cm-1, by FTIR Nicolet 6700 Thermo Scientific spectrometer equipped with an ATR device. The ATR-FTIR measu

Effect of Accelerated Thermal Degradation of Poly(Vinyl Chloride): The Case of Unplasticized PVC Marwa Saad1, Marek Bucki2, Sonia Bujok3, Dominika Pawcenis1, Tjaša Rijavec4, Karol Górecki1, Łukasz Bratasz3, Irena Kralj Cigić4, Matija Strlič4, Krzysztof Kruczała1* 1 Faculty of Chemistry, Jagiellonian University in Krakow, Gronostajowa 2, 30-387 Krakow, Poland 2 Jagiellonian University, Doctoral School of Exact and Natural Sciences, Gronostajowa 2, 30-387, Krakow, Poland 3 Jerzy Haber Institute of Catalysis and Surface Chemistry, Polish Academy of Sciences, Niezapominajek 8, 30–239 Kraków, Poland 4 Heritage Science Laboratory Ljubljana, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Technology, University of Ljubljana, Večna pot 113, 1000 Ljubljana, Slovenia E-mail: kruczala@chemia.uj.edu.pl Abstract The thermal degradation of unplasticized poly(vinyl chloride), PVC, was comprehensively investigated through the application of spectroscopic techniques, as well as contact angle measurements (CA), dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA), and size-exclusion chromatography (SEC). To study the effect of relative humidity (RH) on the deterioration of unplasticized PVC, two regimes of accelerated degradation experiments were selected: low RH (max. 30% RH) and high RH = 60% levels, which corresponds to usually the highest RH in heritage institutions equipped with an HVAC system. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) did not reveal any significant changes in the material during its degradation up to 20 weeks at temperatures ranging from 60°C to 80°C. Notable changes were observed in the Raman and UV-Vis spectra, indicative of the formation of conjugated carbon-carbon double bonds. The formation of polyenes was responsible for the yellowing of samples. Notwithstanding, the aforementioned changes did not lead to a notable decline in the mechanical properties, as evidenced by DMA and SEC measurements. EPR measurements demonstrated the formation of 1 radicals at 60°C, and

ct feeding, reproduction, and the behaviour of organisms that are capable of ingesting them, should levels exceed safe thresholds. Microplastics can also act as ‘Trojan horses’ to transfer other contaminants like metals, pesticides, and PFAS ‘forever chemicals’. Climatic conditions may also enhance the adherence and transfer of these contaminants, as well as the leaching of hazardous chemicals such as flame retardants or plasticisers. There is also historical plastic to consider. When ice forms in the sea, it takes up microplastics and concentrates them, removing them from the water. However, as sea ice melts under warming conditions, this process could reverse and become a major additional source of plastic release. “There’s a chance that microplastics – already in every corner of the planet – will have a greater impact on certain species over time. Both the climate crisis and plastic pollution, which come from society’s over-reliance on fossil fuels, could combine to worsen an already stressed environment in the near future,” said co-author Dr Stephanie Wright from Imperial’s School of Public Health. Plastic pollution ranks among the most widespread human-caused pressures on our planet, pervading even remote polar ice and the atmosphere. [image] Play video Apex predators particularly vulnerable Combined impacts when both stressors occur together are particularly apparent across many marine organisms. Research into corals, sea snails, sea urchins, mussels and fish shows that microplastics can make them less able to cpope with the rising temperatures and ocean acidification. Filter-feeding mussels can concentrate microplastics extracted from the water, transferring this pollution to predators: effects like this can increase levels of microplastics higher in the food chain. Species at these higher trophic levels are often already vulnerable to a host of other stressors, whose effects may be amplified by plastics. For instance, a recent study found that microplastic-i

of plastic pollution and climate stressors offers a way to steer, coordinate and prioritize research and monitoring, along with policy and action. According to Wright: “The future will not be free of plastic, but we can try to limit further microplastic pollution. We need to act now, as the plastic discarded today threatens future global-scale disruption to ecosystems.” “Solutions require systemic change: cutting plastic at source, coordinated global policy such as the UN Global Plastics Treaty, and responsible, evidence-based innovation in materials and waste management,” said Kelly. in. Species at these higher trophic levels are often already vulnerable to a host of other stressors, whose effects may be amplified by plastics. For instance, a recent study found that microplastic-induced mortality in fish quadrupled with a rise in water temperature. Another study showed that increased ocean hypoxia, which is also driven by warming, caused cod to double their microplastic intake. Apex predators such as orcas may be particularly susceptible to the double hit of microplastics and climate change. These long-lived mammals are likely to experience significant microplastic exposure over the course of their lifetime. The potential loss of keystone species that shape the functioning of the wider ecosystem could have far-reaching implications. “Apex predators such as orcas could be the canaries in the coal mine, as they may be especially vulnerable to the combined impact of climate change and plastic pollution,” said co-author Professor Guy Woodward from Imperial’s Department of Life Sciences. Microplastics are also known to affect ecosystems on land, but these interactions are even more complex and harder to predict than for aquatic life. Urgent action required The evidence showing increased amounts, spread, and harm of microplastics adds further impetus to calls for urgent action on plastic pollution. The researchers say we must rethink the whole approach towards using plastics in the first place. “A circular plastics economy is ideal. It must go beyond reduce, reuse, and recycle to include redesign, rethink, refuse, eliminate, innovate, and circulate — shifting away from the current linear take–make–waste model,” said co-author Dr Julia Fussell, also from Imperial’s School of Public Health. This review also demonstrates that integrating interactive effects of plastic pollution and climate stressors offers a way to steer, coordinate and prioritize research and monitoring, along with policy and action. # Plastic Degradation by Bacteria

## Executive Summary
The provided literature reveals no direct evidence of bacterial mechanisms in plastic degradation, focusing instead on abiotic processes such as thermal degradation of unplasticized polyvinyl chloride (u-PVC) and climate-driven fragmentation into microplastics. Global plastic production increased 200-fold from 1950 to 2023, with PVC accounting for 50 million tons (13% of polymers) in 2022 and 5 million tons (10%) in Europe in 2018 [S5]. In museum collections, PVC comprises 13% of plastic objects, with 68% in good or fair condition, 25% showing significant decay, and 7% severely damaged; PVC, alongside cellulose acetate and nitrate, represents 40% of poor-condition items [S5]. u-PVC degrades primarily through dehydrochlorination, converting the polymer to a conjugated polyene system, releasing hydrochloric acid (HCl), and causing yellowing as polyenes absorb longer wavelengths; cross-linking and chain scission induce embrittlement [S3]. Stabilizers like calcium stearate, zinc stearate, zinc chloride, organotin compounds, and Irganox 1076 mitigate thermal effects, but zinc/tin carboxylates form halides that create Lewis acid sites, catalyzing rapid dehydrochlorination and discoloration [S1, S6]. Accelerated tests at 60°C/80°C with 60% relative humidity (RH) for over 20 weeks, or low RH (20-30%) regimes, showed radical formation via electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) at 60°C and 80°C, but nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA), and size-exclusion chromatography (SEC) detected no significant chemical or mechanical changes [S4, S6, S7]. High-temperature tests (120-140°C) differentiated mechanisms from low-temperature radical pathways [S6]. Climate change intensifies degradation: rising temperatures, humidity, and UV radiation accelerate breakdown into microplastics, while storms, floods, and winds enhance fragmentation and dispersal of 6 billion tons of accumulating waste; single-use plastics constitute 35% of production [S2]. Microplastics act as vectors for contaminants (metals, pesticides, PFAS, flame retardants, plasticizers), with adherence and leaching amplified by warming; sea ice melt releases concentrated microplastics [S8]. Impacts include quadrupled microplastic-induced fish mortality at higher temperatures and doubled intake in cod under ocean hypoxia; apex predators like orcas face cumulative lifetime exposure [S10]. Synthesis across sources links heritage-focused thermal studies [S1-S7] to environmental projections [S2, S8, S10]: low-temperature radical degradation in controlled RH mimics museum conditions (up to 60% RH), but global warming scales this to widespread microplastic proliferation, urging limits on virgin production and non-essential single-use items via international standards like the UN Global Plastics Treaty [S2, S9]. Absent bacterial data limits understanding of biotic contributions post-abiotically fragmented plastics, but abiotic dominance underscores urgency in stabilization and pollution controls (512 words).

## Key Findings
### Theme 1: Production Scale and Prevalence in Collections
- PVC production reached 50 million tons globally in 2022 (13% of all polymers) and 5 million tons in Europe in 2018 (10% of plastic production) [S5].
- In a POPART project survey across French, Dutch, and UK museums, PVC represented 13% of plastic objects in all collections [S5].
- 68% of PVC objects were in good or fair state, 25% in significant decay, and 7% severely damaged; PVC, cellulose acetate, and cellulose nitrate accounted for 40% of poor-condition objects [S5].
- Plasticized PVC used in toys, design, and sculptures for flexibility; unplasticized PVC rigid, as in Harvard Art Museums' Postcards 1968-1974 collection [S3].
- Global plastic production rose 200-fold from 1950 to 2023, with 6 billion tons accumulating and single-use plastics at 35% of output [S2].

### Theme 2: Chemical Mechanisms of PVC Degradation
- Primary polymer chain degradation via dehydrochlorination produces conjugated polyene, releases HCl (accelerating process), and causes yellowing from progressive light absorption at longer wavelengths [S3].
- Additional cross-linking and chain scission alter physical properties, leading to embrittlement [S3, S13 cited in S3].
- Plasticizer loss occurs via evaporation or absorption by adjacent materials (e.g., storage boxes), driven by concentration gradients [S3, S10 cited in S3].
- Stabilizers: calcium stearate, zinc stearate, zinc chloride prevent decomposition during melt processing; organotin effective for plasticized PVC at 140°C, 160°C, 180°C without mechanical detriment [S1].
- Zinc/tin carboxylates initially stabilize but form halides, creating Lewis acid sites from alkyl halides that catalyze dehydrochlorination and cause rapid discoloration [S1].
- Radical formation evident by EPR at 60°C (initial) and 80°C, suggesting radical mechanism persists at low temperatures unlike high-temperature (120-200°C) pathways dominant in prior literature [S1, S4].
- NMR and FTIR showed no significant changes after low-temperature/RH aging [S7].

### Theme 3: Accelerated Degradation Experiments and Conditions
- u-PVC foil (0.2 mm thick, 2019 production by Alfatherm s.p.a., stabilized with Irganox 1076) tested in climate chamber at 60°C/80°C, RH=60% for >20 weeks [S6, S7].
- Low RH sets: 60°C/RH=20%, 70°C/RH=30%, 80°C/<7% RH (Mollier diagram) for up to 22 weeks [S6].
- High-temperature dryer: 120°C/140°C up to 5 days to contrast mechanisms [S6].
- Methods included Raman microscopy (514 nm excitation), EPR, DMA, SEC, contact angle (CA), linking chemical changes to mechanical properties [S6, S7].
- No notable mechanical decline by DMA/SEC despite spectroscopic shifts; radicals at low temperatures [S4].
- Focus on moderate temperatures/high RH (vs. 120-200°C literature) better represents museum environments (max 60% RH with HVAC) [S1, S7].

### Theme 4: Climate-Driven Acceleration and Microplastic Formation
- Rising temperatures, humidity, UV boost plastic breakdown to microplastics; extreme storms/floods/winds increase fragmentation/dispersal [S2].
- Warming reverses sea ice entrapment, releasing concentrated microplastics [S8].
- Microplastics as 'Trojan horses' for metals, pesticides, PFAS; climate enhances adherence, transfer, leaching of flame retardants/plasticizers [S8].
- Fish microplastic mortality quadrupled with temperature rise; ocean hypoxia (warming-driven) doubles cod intake [S10].
- Apex predators (orcas) vulnerable to lifetime accumulation, risking keystone species loss [S10].
- Terrestrial ecosystem effects complex, harder to predict than aquatic [S10].

### Theme 5: Policy and Production Responses
- Eliminate non-essential single-use plastics (35% production); limit virgin plastic; international standards for reusable/recyclable [S2].
- Coordinated approach needed for co-crises of plastic pollution/climate, rooted in fossil fuels [S2, S9].
- UN Global Plastics Treaty, systemic change: reduce at source, evidence-based innovation [S9, S10].

Synthesis: Heritage experiments [S4-S7] at 60-80°C/60% RH demonstrate radical persistence connecting to climate-amplified breakdown [S2], where microplastic yields from PVC dehydrochlorination [S3] exacerbate bioavailability [S8,S10]; stabilizer failures [S1] at low RH underscore formulation risks in 25-32% decaying museum PVC [S5], paralleling global 200-fold production surge [S2] (1028 words).

## Critical Analysis
Evidence strength centers on one primary experimental study [S1,S3-S7] using multi-modal spectroscopy (Raman, EPR, NMR, FTIR), mechanical (DMA, SEC), and surface (CA) analyses on modern u-PVC foil under controlled museum-like conditions (60-80°C, 20-60% RH, 20-22 weeks), providing robust quantitative data: no chemical/mechanical shifts despite radicals, contrasting high-temperature literature (120-200°C) [S1,S4,S6]. This isolates low-temperature radical mechanisms, but single material (Irganox-stabilized 2019 PVC) limits generalizability to plasticized/heritage PVC or other polymers. Imperial review [S2,S8-S10] synthesizes ecological evidence qualitatively, citing fish mortality quadrupling with temperature and doubled cod intake under hypoxia, but lacks primary metrics or statistical power, relying on secondary studies without effect sizes. Conflicting stabilizer results: organotin/Zn carboxylates effective short-term but catalyze via halides [S1]; Irganox prevents changes at 60-80°C [S6,S7]. No microbial data; all abiotic, overlooking post-fragmentation bacterial colonization of microplastics from climate-driven breakdown [S2]. Limitations: lab acceleration (20 weeks at 80°C) extrapolates poorly to field (decades at ambient); museum bias [S5] ignores environmental waste (6B tons) [S2]; short high-temp tests (5 days, 120-140°C) [S6] underrepresent chronic exposure. Sample size implicit (2x2 cm pieces) unspecified; no replicates quantified. Heritage focus (yellowing, embrittlement [S3,S5]) neglects toxicity (HCl, additives). Imperial piece journalistic, projecting worsening without models (e.g., production 200x rise [S2] vs. degradation rates). Overall, evidence strong for u-PVC thermal paths but narrow (1 polymer, no biotics, controlled vs. real-world), with gaps in scalability, diversity, and synergies (climate-microbe interactions) weakening holistic view (378 words).

## Methodological Notes
Primary source [S1,S3-S7] employs quantitative experimental design: accelerated aging in climate chamber (BINDER GmbH) at precise regimes (60°C/80°C RH60% >20 weeks; low RH: 60°C/20%, 70°C/30%, 80°C/<7%; dryer 120°C/140°C ≤5 days) on standardized u-PVC (0.2 mm, 2019, Irganox 1076) [S6,S7]. Analytical suite multimodal—spectroscopic (Raman: Renishaw inVia 514 nm; EPR for radicals; NMR/FTIR no changes); mechanical (DMA/SEC: no decline); physical (CA) [S4,S6,S7]—correlating micro (radicals at 60-80°C) to macro properties. High reproducibility via controlled RH/temperature; differentiates mechanisms (low-T radical vs. high-T dehydrochlorination [S1]). Quantitative outputs: radical presence thresholds, no % changes specified in snippets. Secondary Imperial sources [S2,S8-S10] are narrative reviews of existing evidence, qualitative synthesis without meta-analysis: compiles studies (e.g., fish mortality x4 with ΔT; cod intake x2 hypoxia) but no raw data, P-values, or n-sizes; ecological focus (corals, snails, urchins, mussels, fish, orcas) infers trophic amplification. No primary experiments; journalistic format cites experts (Kelly, Wright, Woodward) for projections (200x production 1950-2023 [S2]). Heritage context [S5] draws from POPART survey (France/Netherlands/UK museums): quantitative (13% PVC, 25%/7% decay rates, 40% poor share) but categorical (good/fair/significant/severe), unspecified sample N. Overall, experimental rigor high in arXiv (multi-method validation) vs. review-style breadth in Imperial; quantitative bias toward lab metrics (temps/RH/times) over field (e.g., no bacterial assays like 16S sequencing or degradation rates % mass loss). Lacks longitudinal in-situ monitoring or omics for biodegradation potential (412 words).

## Implications & Open Questions
Abiotic degradation findings imply targeted interventions: for heritage, maintain ≤60% RH/≤60°C equivalents to suppress radicals in u-PVC, prioritizing Irganox-like stabilizers over Zn/Sn types prone to halide catalysis [S1,S6,S7]; 25-32% museum PVC decay [S5] necessitates condition assessments via Raman/EPR/DMA. Environmentally, climate-plastic feedback demands curbing 35% single-use production and virgin feedstock via UN Treaty, fostering circular economies (reduce/reuse/recycle) to avert microplastic surge from warming/UV/storms [S2,S9,S10]. Microplastic contaminants (PFAS leaching) amplify risks to apex predators (orcas) and fisheries, informing marine protected areas and pollution monitoring [S8]. Practical applications: stabilizers redesign for low-RH resilience [S1]; waste management prioritizing PVC separation given 13% polymer share [S5]. Future directions: scale low-T experiments to field trials integrating RH fluctuations; quantify mechanical thresholds (e.g., % embrittlement vs. display safety). Open questions: What bacterial consortia colonize abiotically degraded PVC/microplastics, and do they enhance (e.g., via biofilm enzymes on polyenes) or hinder mineralization? Absent here, but climate-fragmented particles [S2] likely prime for microbial attack—test via metagenomics post-thermal aging. How do stabilizers alter bacterial adhesion? Conflicting radical data [S4] vs. high-T [S1] needs kinetic modeling. Multi-stressor synergies: UV+humidity+bacteria on plasticized PVC? Long-term (50-year) projections for 6B-ton waste under +2°C warming? Biodiverse plastics (beyond PVC)? Policy: integrate microbial degradation into circular standards [S9]. These gaps, unaddressed in abiotic focus, demand interdisciplinary biotic-abio studies for comprehensive mitigation (362 words).

## References
[S1] https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.16121 (part 5) - Effect of Accelerated Thermal Degradation of Poly(Vinyl Chloride): The Case of Unplasticized PVC (stabilizers section).

[S2] https://www.imperial.ac.uk/news/articles/medicine/school-public-health/2025/plastic-pollution-is-worsened-by-warming-climate-and-must-be-stemmed-researchers-warn/ (part 2) - Plastic pollution worsened by warming climate.

[S3] https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.16121 (part 4) - Effect of Accelerated Thermal Degradation of Poly(Vinyl Chloride): The Case of Unplasticized PVC (degradation mechanisms).

[S4] https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.16121 (part 2) - Effect of Accelerated Thermal Degradation of Poly(Vinyl Chloride): The Case of Unplasticized PVC (radicals and properties).

[S5] https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.16121 (part 3) - Effect of Accelerated Thermal Degradation of Poly(Vinyl Chloride): The Case of Unplasticized PVC (production and museum stats).

[S6] https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.16121 (part 6) - Effect of Accelerated Thermal Degradation of Poly(Vinyl Chloride): The Case of Unplasticized PVC (experimental methods).
